description: Both were initially heard calling "pip" which alerted us to their presence. The birds were widely separated, eliminating the possibility of recounting. Of the close to a dozen "Trail's" Flycatchers observed that day at Tamarack, as many as four appeared to be good for Alder, but only two were confirmed with vocalizations. Both were foraging in willows and other thickets low to the ground in typical Willow/Alder fashion. These were rather bulky empids with relatively short, thick bills (for a Trail's). Upper mandible dark while lower mandible was orangish-yellow. Back and crown were noticeably olive-green. Both birds sported narrow but complete white eyerings when seen well (at times very conspicuous, other times thin and hardly detectable). The white throat contrasted noticeably with grayish-green cheeks. Underparts pale with yellow-olive wash. Black tertials with contrasting white edges. The head was rounded or flat, but never appeared to take on the crested appearance of a Willow Flycatcher. Primary projection fairly long for a Trail's Flycatcher. Strong white wingbars tinged with olive.
photographer: Photographer: Jack Bushong, Jack Bushong, Jack Bushong, Jack Bushong
